questionText: A particle moves along the curve $x = 2\cos t$, $y = 5\sin t$, where $t$ is in radians. Find the exact speed of the particle at the instant $t = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$.

steps.explanation: The speed is the length of the velocity vector, so we combine the horizontal and vertical rates using Pythagoras.

steps.description: Differentiate x with respect to t
steps.explanation: Differentiating $2\cos t$ gives the horizontal velocity component.

steps.description: Differentiate y with respect to t
steps.explanation: Differentiating $5\sin t$ gives the vertical velocity component.

steps.description: Insert the exact trig values at t = pi/4
steps.explanation: At $t=\tfrac{\pi}{4}$ sine and cosine are equal, so we can keep everything exact.
Substituting the exact values gives the two components at this instant.
Squaring removes the sign and the surds, leaving simple fractions to add.
Writing $2$ as $\tfrac{4}{2}$ lets us add the fractions to a single value under the root.
The speed is the square root of the summed squares; we now tidy the surd.
Multiplying top and bottom by $\sqrt{2}$ clears the surd from the denominator, giving the tidy exact answer.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: The exact speed at $t=\dfrac{\pi}{4}$ is $\dfrac{\sqrt{58}}{2}$ units per unit time.
